critic_raw:
Section 4 states preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting. This is explicit leakage — the vocabulary derived from test-set clinical notes (later time period) informs what tokens are available in the training vocabulary. For clinical BERT fine-tuning, this affects special token handling and text normalization applied to test-set inputs.

Section 6 describes 'final cross-verification on the test set to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge.' This is test set contamination — if the configuration does not maintain its edge on test, the design implies revisiting hyperparameters. Any reported test metric is contaminated by this decision loop.

The comparison is TF-IDF + logistic regression versus fine-tuned ClinicalBERT. This is not an architecture comparison — it is a representation paradigm comparison (bag-of-words counting vs. contextual pretrained language model). The claim that gains are attributable to the 'transformer architecture' is incorrect; they are attributable to contextual pretraining on biomedical text. A fair architecture comparison would hold representation quality constant (e.g., BioBERT embeddings for both, with a transformer classifier head vs. LR head).

Assigning each patient a single complication label (most severe) discards multi-label information and creates a label priority artifact. For patients with both a minor wound complication and a major cardiac event, the major label dominates. The model is trained to predict the label that trumps all others — but clinically, identifying any complication (including minor ones) may be more valuable than predicting the most severe one.

Training data covers 22 months (months 1-22) and test data covers months 29-36 (7 months). There is a 7-month gap between training and test periods. This gap, combined with temporal ordering, means clinical documentation practices, complication coding, and hospital protocols may shift between training and test periods. The temporal gap is more severe than in many designs — it systematically disadvantages models trained on older documentation patterns.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing tokenizer and vocabulary builder are fit on the entire dataset including test-set clinical notes — test-period vocabulary and text normalization statistics inform the training feature space.
Section 6 describes using the test set to verify hyperparameter configuration performance before finalizing — this is explicit test set contamination for model selection decisions.

The comparison is TF-IDF bag-of-words versus fine-tuned ClinicalBERT — this tests representation paradigm (counting vs. contextual pretraining), not transformer architecture; any observed advantage cannot be attributed to the transformer design.

Single-label per patient (most severe complication) discards multi-label information and trains the model to predict dominance hierarchies rather than complication occurrence — this may be clinically suboptimal and produces a biased label distribution for rare minor complications.

There is a 7-month temporal gap between the end of training (month 22) and the start of test (month 29) — documentation practice changes and protocol evolution during this gap create systematic distribution shift that is not present in adjacent-period temporal splits.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing tokenizer and vocabulary builder are fit on the entire dataset including test-set clinical notes — test-period vocabulary and text normalization statistics inform the training feature space.
Section 6 describes using the test set to verify hyperparameter configuration performance before finalizing — this is explicit test set contamination for model selection decisions.

The comparison is TF-IDF bag-of-words versus fine-tuned ClinicalBERT — this tests representation paradigm (counting vs. contextual pretraining), not transformer architecture; any observed advantage cannot be attributed to the transformer design.

Single-label per patient (most severe complication) discards multi-label information and trains the model to predict dominance hierarchies rather than complication occurrence — this may be clinically suboptimal and produces a biased label distribution for rare minor complications.

There is a 7-month temporal gap between the end of training (month 22) and the start of test (month 29) — documentation practice changes and protocol evolution during this gap create systematic distribution shift that is not present in adjacent-period temporal splits.
